📍Tokyo 🇯🇵 Have you ever heard of Forest Bathing? 
It’s a term emerged in Japan in the 1980s which is called Shinrin Yoku (森林浴) in Japanese. Shinrin is Forest, Yoku means Bathing, the whole phrase means bathing in the forest atmosphere, or taking in the forest through all senses. And it’s not a difficult thing to do at all. It’s simply being in nature, getting yourself reconnected with nature, letting the sounds of the forests, the fragrance of the trees and grasses, the sunlight flickering through the branches, the clean fresh air comfort you. It helps you to ease your stress and anxiety, to relax and take back your energy. 
This reel was shot in Takaosan, Tokyo. If you’re living in Tokyo and you miss nature, this place might be a great choice 🥰  Wherever you’re, this is a small reminder to you to spend time in nature 💚 Have a nice day ✨
📍 Takaosan, Tokyo (高尾山、東京)

This tiny little underground bar was beautiful. The music, the vibes and the cocktails were all 10/10. Mr Ikeda was so humble and spoke a little English plus with our small amount of Japanese so we were able to chat a little as we watched his precision making his unique cocktails. Japanese green peppercorns are only in season for 3 weeks a year and have the most amazing subtle fragrance and flavour plus a numbing sensation not dissimilar to sichuan pepper. This paired with his parent’s ‘natsudaidai’ aka homegrown summer oranges was next level. I’ll never forget this cocktail!!

The 2024 BMW i4 is a sleek and powerful electric Gran Coupe that marks a significant step forward for BMW’s electric vehicle lineup. 
It comes in two main variants: the eDrive40 and the performance-oriented M50. 🔨

The eDrive40 boasts a single electric motor on the rear axle, generating 340 horsepower and 295 lb-ft of torque. This setup allows the i4 eDrive40 to zip from 0 to 60 mph in a brisk 5.4 seconds, according to BMW estimates. With an EPA-estimated range of up to 301 miles on a single charge (using 18-inch wheels), the eDrive40 offers impressive efficiency for daily commutes and longer road trips. 🔧

For those craving exhilarating acceleration, the i4 M50 steps up the game. Packing a dual-motor setup, it cranks out a combined output of 536 horsepower and a staggering 586 Ib-ft of torque. This translates to a scorching 0-60 mph time of 3.7 seconds, placing it amongst the quickest electric vehicles on the market.
However, the M50 sacrifices some range for its performance edge, offering an EPA-estimated range of up to 270 miles. 🛤️

Both variants utilize a high-voltage battery pack that supports DC fast charging. With a focus on performance, efficiency, and luxurious comfort, the 2024 BMW i4 is a compelling option for those seeking a thrilling electric driving experience.

It’s been a minute since I last visited @tokiunderground, and I almost forgot how good their food was. I’m excited to go back and try more of their menu ASAP‼️ Here’s everything I got with @charmcityfoodadventures for our foodie frand date: 

▪️Cloud Shrimp ($12): perfectly fried shrimp served with an addicting kimchi kewpie mayo. 10/10 recommend. This may have been my favorite bite of the evening.
▪️Toki Wings ($12): I couldn’t resist getting this when I saw that it came in a spicy yuzu root beer sauce. So creative and fun!
▪️Mala Garlic Tonkotsu ($20): Easily one of the most unique ramen bowls I’ve ever had. If you’re a fan of spicy, definitely give this a try. This bowl’s made with chashu pork belly, scallions, kale, onsen egg, pickled ginger, whipped garlic, and numbing Sichuan oil. YUM.
▪️Cold Summer Noodle with Crab ($19): if you’re not a fan of hot noodz in the summer, this is a great option. This bowl’s made with ponzu, scallions, omelette, shredded nori, cakes, avocado, and a fermented honey sriracha. You can also get this with chicken or mushroom if you’d prefer!
▪️Pavlova (not on the menu but can be preordered): holy crap this was good. Probably the best pavlova I’ve had, lol. Light, beautiful, and not overly sweet. Highly recommend preordering for special occasions or events!

Additional notes 📝
▪️There’s lots of parking right up front
▪️They have one of the PRETTIEST patios I’ve ever seen 
▪️Their cocktail game’s strong, and the Toki Monster’s a popular option
▪️They have a second location in DC, and they’ve been a Michelin Bib Gourmand since 2018

I loved staying at this hotel!! It’s definitely a mix of traditional yet very modern hotel elements and thats what makes it super unique!☺️💖

Here’s some facts about the hotel!

✨It was built over 110 years ago!

✨Tsubaki St. (the inside of the hotel) is reminiscent of the historical geisha districts of Kyoto known as Hanamachi. 

✨Their rooms are named and decorated after the five elements of nature.

✨Each room has a private onsen!

I was a little worried because the hotel is in the middle of the city, but it wasn’t loud and it feels very private! You also get a complimentary snack or meal from the hotel every night!😋

The nearest train station is about a 5-10 minute walk and there’s also some small hole in the wall restaurants nearby that you can checkout! However it’s not super close to the major city center/tourist spots so definitely keep that in mind!🫶

This underground wagyu-centered omakase is truly something special, offering a dining experience unlike any others in Washington, DC. 

@kappodc located in the basement of a Palisade storefront is a 21-seat establishment that offers an 8-course tasting menu where top quality wagyu is the center piece of every dish. 

Kappo translates to “cut and cook” in Japanese which is what you will witness if you decide to opt-in for a seat at the Chef’s counter. As each course rolled out one after the other, we were consistently astounded by the robust flavors in every dish. 

The creativity of the chefs at Kappo is something to admire, and the commitment to their craft is truly a work of art. We won’t be surprised if we see a Michelin star in their future.

You have an odor fighter right in your liquor cabinet.  I learned this one from my Grandmother.  A simple spray of vodka can eliminate odors on clothing, furniture, upholstery and even mattresses.  

All you need is vodka and a spray bottle - make sure your spray bottle has a mist setting - not just a straight spray.  To spray clothing, focus on the areas that collect the most bacteria and therefore odor - like under the sleeves.  Spray from about 18” away from the clothing in sort of a sweeping motion. Works on mattresses, sofas, and shoes too!!! Vodka kills odor causing bacteria and evaporates without a trace 👏👏 #grandmas #housekeeping #cleaning

Some beautiful hot spring towns in Japan that offer tattoo friendly onsen ♨️

Note: However it doesn’t mean all the accommodations or public onsen are allowed in the area.

Kusatsu Onsen (Gunma Prefecture):
Ranked number one famous onsen town in Japan, Kusatsu has some ryokan and private baths that accept guests with tattoos.

Dogo Onsen (Ehime Prefecture):
One of Japan’s oldest and most famous hot spring town. While the main public bathhouse, Dogo Onsen Honkan, has traditionally had stricter policies regarding tattoos, some ryokan and hotels in the area offer private baths where tattoos are more likely to be accepted. 

Beppu (Oita Prefecture):
Known for its variety of hot springs, Beppu has a few tattoo-friendly onsens. Some public baths and private onsen establishments are more relaxed about tattoos.

Minakami Onsen (Gunma Prefecture):
This town is known for its outdoor baths with beautiful natural surroundings. Some onsen in Minakami are tattoo-friendly.

Kinosaki Onsen (Hyogo Prefecture):
This charming onsen town has some tattoo-friendly ryokan (traditional inns) and public baths. You might need to ask beforehand, but many establishments are becoming more accommodating to tattooed guests.

Tips for Tattoo-Friendly Onsen Visits:
Private Baths:
 Many ryokan and hotels offer private onsen baths that can be reserved for personal use, which are often more accommodating for tattooed guests. Contact Ahead:
Before visiting, always contact the specific onsen or accommodation to inquire about their tattoo policy. This ensures a smooth and enjoyable experience.

For the most accurate and current information, please contact the specific onsen or ryokan directly before your visit to confirm their tattoo policy is the best approach.

Never forget in a tech interview again!👇What is a Websocket and how is it different than HTTPS?

A websocket is a communication protocol that allows real-time, bi-directional data transfer between a client and a server over a single, persistent connection. It’s like having a telephone conversation, where both parties can send messages to each other at any time, without the need for a request-response cycle like in the traditional HTTP model.

WebSocket is efficient, reducing overhead by eliminating HTTP headers, and is ideal for applications requiring real-time updates, such as chat apps, live feeds, or multi-player games. The server can push data to the client as soon as it’s available, without the client having to poll for updates.

To establish a WebSocket connection, the client sends an HTTP request with an “Upgrade” header, and if the server supports WebSocket, the connection is upgraded. From then on, the client and server can send messages using the WebSocket API, simplifying the development of real-time applications.

#tech #technology #stem #websocket #coding

What is the difference between a load balancer and reverse proxy? 

A load balancer and a reverse proxy serve different primary purposes, although they share some similarities. A load balancer focuses on distributing incoming network traffic across multiple backend servers to optimize resource usage, maximize throughput, and minimize response time. On the other hand, a reverse proxy acts as an intermediary between clients and servers, providing an additional layer of security, performing tasks such as SSL termination, caching, compression, and URL rewriting before forwarding client requests to the appropriate server.

#tech #techexpert #stem #technology

What is Kubernetes? 👇

Kubernetes is a tool that helps manage and organize applications running on multiple computers or servers. Think of it as a conductor in an orchestra, making sure all the musicians (applications) are playing together smoothly and efficiently.

It allows you to easily deploy, scale, and manage your applications across a cluster of machines. This means you can run your applications reliably, handle more traffic, and update them without downtime.

In simple terms, Kubernetes makes it easier to run and manage applications in a world where everything is becoming more distributed and complex.

#tech #stem #developer #coding

This restaurant is called Gonpachi. Be careful if you want to visit, because there are multiple locations and they don’t all look exactly the same! This one is located in Nishi-Azabu, just a short walk away from Roppongi area 🇯🇵

What is a container in tech? But explained simply…. 

We hear alot about Docker but there are many other containers to know about 👇

1. LXC (Linux Containers): LXC is a container runtime that provides a virtual environment with its own process and network space. It’s one of the oldest container technologies and forms the foundation for many other container runtimes.

2. LXD: LXD is a container hypervisor that builds on top of LXC. It provides a more user-friendly interface and additional features like live migration and storage management.

3. containerd: containerd is a standalone container runtime that was originally part of the Docker project. It’s a lightweight, portable container runtime that focuses on simplicity and robustness.

4. CRI-O: CRI-O is a container runtime that implements the Kubernetes Container Runtime Interface (CRI). It’s designed to be lightweight and optimized for Kubernetes.

5. Podman: Podman is a daemonless container engine that’s compatible with the Docker CLI. It allows users to run containers as regular users, without requiring root privileges.

6. Rkt (Rocket): Rkt is a container engine developed by CoreOS (now part of Red Hat). It’s designed to be composable, secure, and compatible with the App Container (appc) specification.

7. Windows Containers: Windows Containers is Microsoft’s container technology that allows running Windows applications in isolated environments on Windows servers.

👇What is the difference between a forward and reverse proxy? 

Reverse Proxy:
- Load balancing: Distributes traffic across multiple servers
- Increased security: Acts as a single entry point, hiding backend servers
- Caching: Improves performance by serving cached content
- SSL termination: Handles SSL encryption, reducing load on backend servers
- Real-world example: Netflix uses a combination of NGINX and Amazon Web Services (AWS) Elastic Load Balancer (ELB) as a reverse proxy to handle massive traffic and ensure smooth content delivery to its users worldwide.

Forward Proxy:
- Anonymity: Hides client IP address from the target server
- Access control: Allows or blocks access to specific websites or content
- Caching: Reduces bandwidth usage and improves performance for frequently accessed content
- Content filtering: Blocks inappropriate or malicious content based on predefined rules
- Real-world example: Many organizations, such as schools and businesses, use forward proxies like Squid or FortiGate to control and monitor internet access, block inappropriate content, and protect their network from external threats.

I’m sorry but I don’t understand the hype around Hakone when within just 2 hours from Tokyo, you can easily access several serene onsen towns via Japan’s efficient train system. The place is already overcrowded, with long lines for buses and cable cars (and that poor torrii gate by the lake…) 

Exploring lesser-known onsen towns near Tokyo offers a more peaceful and relaxed experience without the crowds and queues (I still advise to skip PH and weekends). So why not venture out and discover other places instead of going to popular sites? 😌🌿

📍Yugawara onsen, Kanagawa Prefecture / 湯河原温泉、神奈川県
🚊1 hour from Tokyo Station to Yugawara Station (+15 minutes by bus or taxi from the station to the onsen town)
📝Visit for the retro ryokans (Japanese inns), great cuisine and waterfalls 

📍Ito city, Shizuoka Prefecture / 伊東市、静岡県
🚊1 hour 30 minutes from Tokyo Station (most ryokans with onsen are near Minami Ito Station)
📝Book accommodation with a kashikiri onsen (貸切温泉）- private bath if you have tattoos. 

📍 Atami city, Shizuoka Prefecture/熱海市、静岡県
45 min from Tokyo Station to Atami Station 
📝Visit for retro kissaten (喫茶店） - coffee houses, fireworks during summer and lively shopping street near Atami Station

Why waste your money on a Japan trip.. when there’s a better way!

Japan doesn’t have to be an expensive holiday. In fact, it can be quite affordable, train travel and accommodation often the big ticket items.

Looking for ways to make your dollar stretch further?

We’ve popped together a few travel hacks you might like to add to the list ⬇️ 

✨ WAYS TO SAVE IN JAPAN ✨ 

🛂 Buy a one day metro pass: These are available in Tokyo, Kyoto, Osaka and other cities. Usually limited to just the metro and not the wide range of privately owned lines, they let you travel unrestricted on the extensive subway routes for a full day. You can buy online 

🍣 Try hot dishes at convenience stores: 7-11, Family Mart, and Lawson convenience stores have a lot of delicious pre-made meals for under 500 JPY. They have microwaves available so you can heat and go!

🍱 Buy food before grocery stores close: A lot of supermarkets in Japan discount their fresh food after 8pm, sometimes slashing prices by up to 50%. Keep an eye out for Maruetsu, Izumiya and Seiyu chains

🍜 Eat curry, ramen, and donburi: We lived off these three foods. Curry bowls and donburi (bowls of meat and rice) are the best ways to eat affordable meals that are really delicious and filling

🎟️ Buy a Tokyo Museum Grutto Pass: It gives you unlimited access to more than 100 museums and facilities in Tokyo. You can purchase online

🪪 Buy a Pasmo or Welcome Suica train card: Load these up with money which is automatically deducted as you travel from station to station. You can also use it at vending machines and to pay at convenience stores

🛜 Buy a data-only SIM card: Purchase this online, ready for collection when you arrive at Kansai International Airport (for Osaka and Kyoto) & Tokyo’s Narita Airport. Or buy & activate an e-sim before you leave home

🚌 Take a night bus: Buses are cheaper than trains and a night bus will also save on accomodation costs. The cheapest rates are weekdays from Monday to Thursday

What do you think? 

📌 save & share to help you plan your Japan holiday 

📍 Katamachi, Kanazawa

OYAKODON ✨ rice cooker series ✨ continues and this time it’s in time for Mother’s Day 🥰  This Japanese dish is a chicken and egg dish which translates to parent and child. It’s traditionally not made in a rice cooker, but if you want a simple easy peasy way to make it all at once, follow Jordan’s recipe 😁👍 

The full non- rice cooker recipe is in our Cooking With The CrunchBros cookbook on 142 ✌️ 

What you need:
2 cups rice (plus the amount of water your rice cooker suggests)
1/4 cup water 
1/2 lb chicken
1/2 onion
1 tbsp hondashi
4 tspn sugar
2.5 tbsp mirin
3.5 tbsp soy sauce 

Garnish with shredded nori(dried seaweed) and benishouga(pickled ginger)

ENJOY!!

※辻利抹茶ミルクを使用

🫕作り方
①耐熱ボウルに生クリームを入れ、600Wで30〜40秒加熱します。
②温めた生クリームに抹茶を入れて混ぜる
③クリームチーズを炊飯器に入れて、ゴムベラで柔らかくする
④砂糖を入れて、またゴムベラで滑らかになるくらい柔らかくする
⑤卵を1個ずつ入れ、よく混ぜる
⑥手順②で作った抹茶と生クリームを混ぜたものを入れて、さらに混ぜる
⑦炊飯器で通常炊飯（ケーキモードがあれば45分）
⑧炊き上がったら、粗熱を取って炊飯器から取り出して、冷蔵庫で2時間以上冷やす
・動画のチーズケーキは2時間ほど冷蔵庫に入れました🥶
　
☝️ここがポイント💡
・クリームチーズは常温で3時間くらい置くか、レンジで30秒すると柔らかくなるよ🔥
・クリームチーズがダマになりやすいから、砂糖、卵は一気に入れない方が良いよ
・一つずつ丁寧に混ぜていけばダマになりません。

⚠️注意
・一度の炊飯で十分加熱されていない場合は様子を見ながら追加で加熱してね。
・釜から取り出しにくい人は作業はボウルでやって、おかまにバターを引いておいてね
・炊飯器の種類によってはケーキが作れない場合があるので、説明書をよく確認してね
・何回やってもドロドロの場合は、炊飯器がケーキ作りに適していない、もしくは古い機種かもしれないので、フライパンで焼き直してみてください。

Oyakodon (親子丼) is a classic Japanese dish traditionally made by simmering chicken and onions in a sweet and savory broth made of dashi, soy sauce, and mirin. Beaten eggs are then poured over, and it’s all served over a bowl of rice.

Comment RICE for a link to my recommended rice cookers! Cook this in any induction rice cooker and it should work.

Rice Cooker Oyakodon 

2 servings

Ingredients:
- 1 cup rice + the regular amount of water you normally use for your rice
- 1 lb chicken thigh, diced
- 1/4 yellow onion, thinly sliced
- 1 tablespoon soy sauce
- 1 teaspoon hondashi
- 1 teaspoon mirin
- Pinch of sugar
- 2-3 eggs
- Green onions and sesame seeds for garnishing

Instructions:
1. Wash your rice, and add to your rice cooker with water.
2. To a bowl, add your soy sauce, hondashi, mirin, and sugar. Mix to combine.
3. Add your diced chicken and thinly sliced onions and toss to coat in the sauce.
4. Put onion & chicken mixture directly over the rice in the rice cooker, being sure to pour over any of the remaining sauce. Set aside the bowl to use next step.
5. Cook as you normally would cook rice. While it’s cooking, to your bowl, crack your 2 eggs and beat with chopsticks or a fork for just 20 seconds.
6. Immediately after the rice is done cooking, quickly open the rice cooker, pour your egg mixture over the chicken and rice and shut it again quickly. Turn on the rice cooker again (on regular rice setting) and allow to cook for 2 minutes.
7. Open rice cooker and serve, garnishing with green onions and roasted sesame seeds.

The Porsche 911 GT3 is a track-focused iteration of the legendary Porsche 911 sports car, meticulously crafted for high-performance driving enthusiasts. Under the hood lies a potent naturally aspirated flat-six engine, delivering over 500 horsepower in most iterations. Its advanced chassis and suspension system, often featuring technologies like rear-wheel steering, ensure razor-sharp handling and agility. 

Aerodynamic enhancements, including a prominent rear wing and front splitter, provide significant downforce for improved stability at high speeds. Inside, the focus remains on the driver, with supportive seats, premium materials, and minimal distractions. Renowned for its track prowess, the GT3 excels in acceleration, cornering, and braking, making it a favorite among both amateur drivers and professional racers. 

Porsche occasionally releases limited-edition models with enhanced performance and exclusive features, further cementing the GT3's status as an automotive icon.

This is MATOKA's favorite authentic Japanese dining experience in Osaka that you do not want to miss! 

In one of my previous videos, I talked about how networking got me to a private omakase experience. That was frankly one of the best experiences that I had in Osaka. And I got to know the chef and owner of this establishment, Yu-san, and we decided that it would be great to share this Hidden gem with all of you. 

There are so many great restaurants in the Dotonbori area, but smaller and more hidden restaurants are the ones that usually have the best food. And let me tell you this place is definitely hidden, so if you are going to book a reservation here, make sure to go early so you're able to find this place on time. 

You can make a reservation by DM on Instagram at @ryouribito

5 Unique Hotels in Japan worth checking out📝

1. Villa Fontaine Grand Haneda Airport 
If you are arriving to Tokyo late or flying out early morning the next day then stay overnight here for easy flight out 

2. Hotel Groove Shinjuku 
Located in the middle of Shinjuku, perfect for food & shopping. The “artist room” was the one we stayed at, biggest room we ever stayed at in Japan, the price is comparable to a base room in USA or Australia, except this is better!

3. Onsen Ryokan Yuen Sapporo 
If you are in Hokkaido, Sapporo during the winter season – this place is conveniently located, also since we are a big onsen nerd, anything with onsen is such a bonus, its heavenly when you dip in😌

4. Disneyland Hotel in Tokyo 
There are a lot of character rooms here, but the one we tried was the Beauty and the Beast room, some perks you get when staying here is early entry tickets to Disneyland before the public comes in, pretty cool😊 

5. The Millenials in Kyoto 
One good thing about capsule hotel is its affordable vs other accommodations. This hotel is great if you want to wake up early for places like Fushimi Inari, Ninenzaka, Kiyomizu Dera.

If you know any other unique hotels in Japan do let us know!

I caught my daughter washing my cast iron with a generous amount of soap and I almost fainted lol. I then realized that it was my fault, I didn’t actually tell her that  Soap and cast iron are sworn enemies.  If you do wash yours with soap, please don’t as soap would effectively wash away the cure that you worked so hard to build. 
When put it back on the stove, the heat on the surface of a cast iron pan easily reaches upwards of 300 degrees, which is high enough to kill any bacteria that don’t get washed away. 

Next, I will share how to wash and make stainless steel pans non stick!

❌ THINGS TO AVOID IN JAPAN & ALTERNATIVES - Part 1

Japan has always been a very popular place to visit. But now more than ever, the country is struggling with over tourism.

If you love people-watching, by all means, still head along to these iconic landmarks in Japan.

If you’re seeking a quieter alternative, we’ve listed them here👇 

❌ AVOID: Visiting Fushimi Inari Shrine in Kyoto. A visit during the day is a huge mistake. It’s super crowded and hard to find a quiet space
✅ ALTERNATIVE: Visit Hie-jinja Shrine in Tokyo instead. It looks very similar, you can visit during the day and it is far less crowded

❌ AVOID: Arashiyama bamboo forest in Kyoto. It attracts hundreds of tourists and tour groups each day. It’s hard to escape the crowds
✅ ALTERNATIVE: Adashino Nenbutsuji Temple Bamboo Forest is a much less crowded alternative in Kyoto. It resembles Arashiyama so much, it’s hard to tell them apart in photos

❌ AVOID: Tokyo Skytree. It’s a tad expensive, often crowded and hard to get a good view or take a pic, especially at peak hours.
✅ ALTERNATIVE: Tokyo Metropolitan Government Building. It’s free to enter and the observation deck is much more spacious, so you have more window room and walking space 

❌AVOID: Golden Gai Tokyo. It’s laneways with glowing lanterns leading into tiny shanty-style bars tucked behind alleyways is stacks of fun. But a lot of bars charge entry fees (up to $20) just to sit for one drink
✅ ALTERNATIVE: Nonbei Yokocho, Shibuya. Here, you can get your Japanese speakeasy bar fix without the ridiculous prices or crowds. 

❌ AVOID: Sensoji Temple’s Asakusa Nakamise. This is the strip of snack and souvenir shops leading up to the iconic Sensoji Temple. What used to be a cultural experience has turned into more of a cattle march
✅ ALTERNATIVE: Ameyoko street market. Right near Ueno Station, it’s like Tokyo’s perfect mix of a garage sale and flea market

We visited the popular spots and honestly loved them just as much as the alternatives. 

Your thoughts? 

Crowds or no crowds?

🤗 We’ll be posting more Japan tips soon…

THIS is how I have earned Platinum Pro or Executive Platinum status on American Airlines ⬇️ 

Enter: the AAdvantage eShopping Portal! 

Did you know that for any purchases you’re making online, you can earn more points and miles by going through a shopping portal. And no, you don’t even need an AA credit card to do this. There are plenty of people who get PP or EP status JUST by doing this. 

For AA Gold status, you need to earn 40,000 Loyalty Points in the qualifying period from March 1, 2024 to February 28, 2025.

Some of my favorite ways to earn more AA Loyalty Points for status are:
- Meal Kits: Hello Fresh, Blue Apron, Factor, Green Chef
-🍷 subscriptions: Laithwaites, First Leaf, National Geographic, WSJ, Winc, etc.
- Motley Fool & Million Acres - no, I don’t use them. I bought both last year for $150 total and earned 16,800 LPs. 
- PetsMart - wait until it’s 6x or more and buy what you need! 
- Apple - wait for 4x and buy what you need 
- Viator - I book most of our tours for trips through Viator and wait until it’s 10-12x to do so. 

With AA status, you can get free seats in Main Cabin Extra, free upgrades to business/first class, free checked bags, able to use the priority lines, One World elite status, and more!

Okay, you’re a huge Pokemon fan on your way to Japan, how do you reserve a seat at Pokemon Cafe? Is it worth the hassle?

1️⃣ Decide on location. There are two cafes – in Tokyo and Osaka, I’ve only been to second one and my experience was pretty cool.

2️⃣ Reserve a table. Online reservation opens every day at 6:00PM (Japan time zone) 31 day before your desired reservation day. The problem is, EVERYTHING GETS BOOKED WITHIN FIRST MINUTE😅 During our last trip, we had 5 days in Osaka, so basically we had 5 days a month before the trip to try our luck, and we only succeeded on the 5th attempt. Here’s some tips: 
• www.pokemoncenter-online.com/cafe/en/reservation.php — this is the website where you make reservation.
• 1st you have to choose the location. Do it before 6 PM😅
• Agree to terms, choose number of guests. Then you’ll see a calendar with no clickable dates. You just stare intensely for a while until the clock hits 6 PM. Refresh page if needed. 
• If you was quick enough you’ll see a blue clickable date. Click on that. Than you’ll have to choose a time slot, but to be honest, you just click on any available to make it quick. I’ve made it to this part couple of times but that I just got thrown out since everything was already booked. BUT if you do get to the next step you’ll need to do some prior work on your browser setting to make it to the finish line.
• BEFORE RESERVATION PROSEES EVEN STARTS, ADJUST AUTO-FILL ON YOUR BROWSER SETTINGS. This was a game changer in our case. We had auto-fill for the full name, phone number and mail. And when we got it through the time slots, our reservation was completed within seconds. We received an email with the details about our visit.

🚨Don’t get upset if you wasn’t lucky to reserve a seat in time, there is also two more way to get to the cafe. First, occasionally check the website for opens time slots. Second, you can go and ask about available seats on the spot.

6. 네즈코 팝통-올해도 등장!저번에는 오후되면 없었는데 올해는 비교적 재고가 많으 듯 해요!

<경험 정리>
1. 굿즈판매점에서 하오리는 구입안하더라도 시착해보고 인증샷도 찍을 수 있어요! 그치만 전  잠깐 입는 것만으로는 부족해서 구입했어여…
2.  렌고쿠식당에서는 개인적으로 함바그가 가장 맛있었음! (케이크랑 야키카레는 2년전에 먹었었는데 생각보다 별로였음ㅠ)
3. 싱글라이더로 2번탔는데 대기시간이 1시간20분/40분정도였음.

TOP 5 PLACES TO VISIT OUTSIDE OF TOKYO🔽

📌Save and 📥add to your next trip itinerary to Tokyo 🇯🇵🗾

Follow us for more Japan trip tips and recommendations!!

DM US for Tours with Local Japanese People! (Transportation, tour guide, photographer, language support icluded)

1. Nagano 長野市

If you’re tired of crowds then head to Nagano! Explore the temples route through the city and make sure to try the delicious sauce katsudon, a breaded and fried pork cutlet covered in the most delicious sauce 

2. Miyajima 宮島

Predominantly known for the iconic torii gate ⛩️ at Itsukushima Shrine, there’s so much more to do on Miyajima than just see this icon. Explore Momijidani Park and take the cable car up the mountain for the most amazing views over the Seto Inland Sea 

3. Hiroshima 広島

Hiroshima is predominantly known for WWII history and the A-bomb, and for those reasons it is absolutely worth a visit. Make sure to visit the Memorial Park and see the Eternal Flame, but also make sure to explore this beautiful city to see how it has been rebuilt! Hiroshima is more than just a day trip 

4. Kanazawa 金沢

Kanazawa is an absolutely beautiful city in Ishikawa prefecture and is home to one of Japan’s most beautiful gardens. Whether you visit as a day trip from Toyama or even Kyoto, or you stay in Kanazawa to explore for longer, this is a must on any Japan itinerary!

5. Toyama 富山

Toyama is a huge city that most people have never heard of the city is lovely and perfectly located for day trips within Toyama prefecture and nearby prefectures, such as Nagano and Ishikawa. Make sure to go on a day trip to Kurobe Gorge to experience the beauty of the Japanese Alps!

The first time I lost the ability to speak, it lasted three days. I had NO IDEA if it was ever coming back. It was really hard for both me and Colton to cope with everything happening without being able to communicate. 

The third day was Easter Sunday, 2021. We went to church. The hymn was announced. We stood up and I mouthed the words I could not sing. 

But then something weird happened. If I tried to speak the words still weren’t there, but when I tried to sing it felt like I could. I started singing. It was the most liberating feeling I ever felt. 

I tugged Colton’s sleeve and put my lips up to his ear. He got chocked up, his strong voice faltering with emotion, my weak voice soaring with praise. When we sat down again the words were gone, but the moment remained. 

I thought: that’s what singing in heaven feels like. 

Easter is coming again soon and here I am, three years later, still losing the ability to speak. It’s mentally and physically difficult to lose so many functions over and over, but Easter reminds me—just as Jesus is resurrected, and just as he resurrected my voice that Sunday three years ago, he’s going to resurrect my body and soul one day too in complete perfection. 

One day, I’ll speak and walk and move with perfect freedom. But I think the first thing I’ll do when I have my perfect legs back is fall to my knees in worship, and the first thing I’ll say when I have a perfect voice is praise to Jesus. 

He’s the reason I have hope and joy in the middle of the hardship. That doesn’t mean it’s easy or I don’t get discouraged, it means that I know as hard as this is, one day my suffering will be turned to glory because Jesus has died for me and saved me. 

FND is unique because I have to learn how to lose the same things over and over. But all the things I’m learning to lose were never mine to begin with. 

The Lord gives and the Lord takes away. Blessed be the name of then Lord.

This Method is the key, but there’s many more to a conversation🔑 ⬇️

The F.O.R.G.E method, this is your secret weapon for making sure conversations never get that awkward silence and after mastering it you will never run out of things to say.

 F is for Family
This is your go to for starting a conversation because everyone’s got some kind of family. It’s relatable and can open up a ton of pathways for your chat. You could ask things like, “Do you have any siblings?” or
“What’s a crazy family tradition you guys have?”

O is for occupation 
This one is huge because people spend most of their day at work. Dive into questions like, “What do you do for a living?” or “What’s your dream job?” You’re getting to understand their ambitions, passions, and maybe even what they’re hustling towards outside of their day job.

R is for Recreation
This is all about fun, hobbies, and what they do to chill. Ask things like, “What do you like to do for fun?” or “Got any hobbies that you’re really into?” This part of the conversation is crucial because it’s all about what makes them tick when they’re off the clock.

G is for Geography
These ones about where they’re from, places they’ve lived, traveled to, or dream of seeing. Ask things like,
“Have you traveled anywhere interesting lately?” or
“Is there a place you’d love to visit someday?”

E is for Experience
Last is the gold mine of storytelling. You’re looking for those memorable moments, lessons learned, or just something interesting. Ask things like, “What’s an unforgettable experience you’ve had?” or “Have you learned anything important lately?”

Remember the 🔑 to making this work is genuinely listen, engage, and expand on what the other person is sharing. A conversations can come organically. Based on how to chemistry 🧪 is going. Never overthink the conversation or that your asking questions. Start conversations with talking about your experience. Good or bad and see if that person react and chime into it. There’s two more key understanding and circumstance. A current situation or job doesn’t define the person

Review 👇
In your country, you might say “thank you” back when you pay at a restaurant, but in Japan, we normally don’t say that.

We say “ごちそうさまでした”. It means ‘Thank you for the meal.” We also use it when we are finished eating at home and sometimes at restaurants.

So remember when you travel to Japan and pay at a restarutnat, say ごちそうさまでした when they say ありがとうございました

This was such a special experience that reminded us to find beauty in imperfection 💕 We’ll be thinking of it every time we use these dishes! Thank you @kintsugi_link and @prettypass.inc ☺️

This is the traditional style of Kintsugi using natural materials, which takes longer than the quick epoxy version that you might find elsewhere. Therefore, to repair a dish from start to finish requires multiple sessions as the lacquer needs time to dry between layers. However, there are options if you’re short on time. 

Here are the options you can enroll in:
- a single session for learning the techniques only
- a single session where learn the techniques, complete the final steps of applying lacquer and gold, and take the dishware home (as shown in this video). 
- a multi-session course, where you’ll get to do every single step from beginning to end!

English workshops are available upon request via the website contact form (minimum of 2 participants).

The carpal tunnel is a narrow passageway between the carpal bones in the wrist and the transverse carpal ligament on the palmar side of the wrist. The median nerve and the finger and thumb flexor tendons run through the tunnel into the hand.

If any extra pressure is placed on the median nerve (usually from inflammation), numbness, tingling, and pain can occur in the thumb, pointer finger, and middle fingers.

Carpal tunnel syndrome is usually caused by repetitive activities. It can also be caused by keeping the hand and wrist in a position that pinches the nerve for a long period of time (making a tight fist, keeping wrist flexed).

Treatment involves improving hand and finger strength, improving grip strength, and improving forearm and wrist strength and mobility. 

These are a few of the exercises I like to use to improve hand and finger mobility and reduce carpal tunnel symptoms.

Comment Carpal Tunnel if you have numbness/tingling in your hand and you need these!

If you liked the roasted feta tomato soup, try this broccoli and cheese version - it’s so creamy so so good!! 🥦🥣

INGREDIENTS
1 (8-ounce) feta chunk
4 cups broccoli florets
1 pound potatoes, peeled & chopped
1 onion, chopped
4-5 sprigs thyme
1 bulb garlic, sliced at the top 
½ teaspoon salt 
1 teaspoon black pepper
¼ cup olive oil 
4 cups vegetable broth 
INSTRUCTIONS
Preheat the oven to 400°F. Line a large baking sheet with parchment paper.
Place the feta, broccoli, potatoes, onion, thyme and garlic in on the prepared sheet pan. Season everything with black pepper and drizzle with olive oil.
Bake in the preheated oven for 30 minutes, or until the vegetables are tender and the feta is melty. 
Remove the roasted garlic bulb and set aside until cool enough to handle.
Transfer the roasted vegetables and feta into a blender. Carefully squeeze the roasted garlic into the blender and add the vegetable broth. Blend for 2 minutes, or until smooth and creamy.

How To Properly Wash your Fruits and Vegetables: 

1. Put your fruits and veggies into a bowl and add a couple of teaspoons of baking soda. 

2. Fill the bowl up with cold water and let everything sit for at least fifteen minutes. 

3. Dump that nasty water and rinse your produce as usual before storing it in an air-tight container. 

It doesn’t take much time, but it’ll help remove all the pesticides, dirt, and whatever else might be on your produce. 

That’s how you can really get your fruits and veggies clean. No B.S. 

- Chef Mike

(20M views!) Two creative lemonade recipes that you might have never seen! 

🍋The 1st lemonade recipe:
❤️To make the rice cooker lemonade (for a 500 ml glass jar)
*mini multi-crystal rock sugar 600-700g
*lemon 6 pcs (cleaned and sliced)
In the rice cooker (or slow cooker), add in a layer of sugar, then a layer of lemon slices; repeat the process and finish with the sugar;
Slow cook 2 hours, and then keep warm 7-9 hours;
After cooling, store it in a clean glass jar, and chill in the fridge.
 
🍋🍋The 2nd  lemonade recipe:
❤️🍯To make (the lemon honey)
*peel of 4 lemons
*sugar 400g
*lemon juice 70g
*water 200
❤️🥤To make the lemonade (1 serving)
*lemon honey 40 ml
*lemon slice 3 pcs
*sparkling water/water 300-500ml
*ice cubes (optional) 
Mix the lemon honey with water first to let it melt, then add in ice cubes if desired.

This roasted feta soup is inspired by the butternut squash I made a few months ago, but this one is made with red peppers and tomatoes. It’s so creamy so so good!! 🍅❤️

INGREDIENTS
1 (8-ounce) feta chunk
1 red pepper, chopped
4 Roma tomatoes, chopped
1 onion, chopped
2 sprigs rosemary
1 bulb garlic, sliced at the top 
Freshly cracked black pepper
¼ cup olive oil 
4 cups vegetable broth 
INSTRUCTIONS
Preheat the oven to 400°F. Line a large baking sheet with parchment paper.
Place the feta, peppers, tomatoes, onion, rosemary and garlic in on the prepared sheet pan. Season everything with black pepper and drizzle with olive oil.
Bake in the preheated oven for 45 minutes, or until the vegetables are tender and the feta is melty. 
Remove the roasted garlic bulb and set aside until cool enough to handle.
Transfer the roasted vegetables and feta into a blender. Carefully squeeze the roasted garlic into the blender and add the vegetable broth. Blend for 2 minutes, or until smooth and creamy. 
Serve with grilled cheese for the best combo!

Heading to Japan for the first time? No worries!

We’ve put together the ultimate list of what to pack for your Japan trip

Even if you have been to Japan before, it doesn’t hurt to go through this check list again

THE ULTIMATE JAPAN TRIP PACKING LIST ⬇️ 

🧻TISSUES AND WET WIPES: Carry tissues, a handkerchief or wet wipes. There’s rarely paper towel and not a lot hand dryers in bathrooms

👕PROPER CLOTHING FOR TEMPLES & SHRINES: Dress respectfully for temples and shrines. Cover your shoulders and knees. Long pants and skirts are best. Remember to remove shoes before entering 

🎒BACKPACK OR LIGHT BAG: There are no public rubbish bins in Japan. Take a backpack or light bag and throw your rubbish in bins at convenience stores or designated areas 

🔌 POWER ADAPTORS: Grab a couple of 100v power adaptors from your local luggage shop before your trip to Japan. They’re hard to find once you’re there. For North American products, unless you have 3 prongs/pins, your electronics should work fine

👟COMFORTABLE SHOES: You easily walk 10-20,000 steps a day in Japan. Pack comfy shoes and make sure you break them in before you leave home

🔋POWER PACKS: Take a couple of portable chargers if you can. We used Google maps ALL THE TIME. It zaps your battery fast

🛜 BUY AN ESIM OR SIM CARD: Wifi is everything in Japan. You use it to navigate, buy tickets, translate and look up info. Purchase a SIM card or an esim and load up with data before you leave home. We used SimsDirect

📌 Save this check list so you can come back to it when you’re booked for Japan and ready to go ✈️ 

Anything we’ve forgotten? Drop your suggestions in the comments below
